title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. At the very least, if no other decision was made, the alms-house would need to accept him. I requested an interview after that. He was not charged with anything shameful and was peaceful and inoffensive in all his conduct, so they had let him roam unrestrained around the prison, particularly in the enclosed grassy courtyard. That's where I discovered him, standing solo in the most tranquil yard, his face toward a tall wall, while I believed I saw the eyes of killers and robbers peering out at him from the narrow openings of the jail windows all around. Bartleby! I know who you are, he said, without glancing back, and I have nothing I want to say to you.  
B. In any case, if nothing else was decided, the alms-house had to take him in. I asked to meet with him then. Since he was not accused of anything disgraceful and was calm and harmless in his behavior, they had allowed him to wander freely around the prison, especially in the fenced grass yard. That's where I found him, standing alone in the quietest yard, facing a high wall, while I thought I saw the eyes of murderers and thieves peering at him from the narrow slits of the jail windows all around. Bartleby! I recognize you, he said, without turning around, and I have nothing to say to you.
C. If nothing else was settled, the alms-house had to take him in, as a last resort. I asked for a meeting after that. He was under no disreputable accusations and was calm and harmless in all his ways, so they had allowed him to wander freely around the prison, especially in the fenced grass yard. That's where I found him, standing alone in the quietest yard, facing a high wall, while I thought I saw the eyes of murderers and thieves peering at him from the narrow slits of the jail windows all around. Bartleby! I recognize you, he said, without turning around, and I have nothing I wish to say to you.
D. At all events, if nothing else could be decided upon, the alms-house must receive him. I then begged to have an interview. Being under no disgraceful charge, and quite serene and harmless in all his ways, they had permitted him freely to wander about the prison, and especially in the inclosed grass-platted yard thereof. And so I found him there, standing all alone in the quietest of the yards, his face towards a high wall, while all around, from the narrow slits of the jail windows, I thought I saw peering out upon him the eyes of murderers and thieves. Bartleby! I know you, he said, without looking round,and I want nothing to say to you.
Answer:

He was not charged with anything shameful and was peaceful and inoffensive in all his conduct, so they had let him roam unrestrained around the prison, particularly in the enclosed grassy courtyard. Bartleby! I know who you are, he said, without glancing back, and I have nothing I want to say to you.
